inline_review:
commit 2fe50c13aebc88839d89dd3cdd5944c18c25dc9b
Author: Liew Rui Yan <aethernet65535@gmail.com>

mm/damon: add synchronous commit for commit_inputs

This commit modifies the commit_inputs module parameter callback to
synchronously apply parameters using damon_call() instead of relying on
asynchronous validation in the kdamond thread.

Link: https://lore.kernel.org/20260318153731.97470-1-aethernet65535@gmail.com

diff --git a/mm/damon/lru_sort.c b/mm/damon/lru_sort.c
--- a/mm/damon/lru_sort.c
+++ b/mm/damon/lru_sort.c
[ ... ]
> @@ -349,18 +348,51 @@ static int damon_lru_sort_apply_parameters(void)
[ ... ]
> +static int damon_lru_sort_commit_inputs_store(const char *val,
> +					      const struct kernel_param *kp)
> +{
> +	bool commit_inputs_request;
>  	int err;
> +	struct damon_call_control control = {
> +		.fn = damon_lru_sort_commit_inputs_fn,
> +	};
>  
> -	if (!commit_inputs)
> +	if (!val) {
> +		commit_inputs_request = true;
> +	} else {
> +		err = kstrtobool(val, &commit_inputs_request);
> +		if (err)
> +			return err;
> +	}
>  
> -	err = damon_lru_sort_apply_parameters();
> -	commit_inputs = false;
> -	return err;
> +	if (!commit_inputs_request)
>  		return 0;
>  
> +	/*
> +	 * Skip damon_call() if ctx is not initialized to avoid
> +	 * NULL pointer dereference.
> +	 */
> +	if (!ctx)
> +		return -EINVAL;

Does this break module loading if commit_inputs=Y is provided as a boot
parameter or via a modprobe configuration?

During module initialization, parse_args() executes module parameter
callbacks before the module's init function runs.

Since ctx is initialized in the module's init function, won't ctx be NULL
when this callback fires, causing an -EINVAL return that aborts the
entire module load?

> +
> +	err = damon_call(ctx, &control);

Could this synchronous call break backward compatibility for userspace
scripts?

Previously, writing Y to commit_inputs while the monitor was disabled
(enabled=N) would silently succeed and apply the parameters on the next
start.

If the kdamond thread is not running, won't damon_call() detect
!damon_is_running(ctx) and return -EINVAL, causing writes to fail when
the monitor is off?

Since this is a module parameter callback, the global param_lock mutex is
held during its execution. Does calling damon_call() here cause system-wide
latency spikes?

damon_call() blocks on wait_for_completion() waiting for the kdamond thread,
which only checks its call list once per sample interval.

Will this stall all other tasks attempting to read or write built-in module
parameters for up to the duration of the DAMON sampling interval?

Is there a race condition here with the kdamond thread termination that
could result in a permanent deadlock?

When kdamond_fn() exits, it flushes pending controls and then sets
ctx->kdamond to NULL.

If a concurrent sysfs write triggers damon_call() after the controls are
flushed but before ctx->kdamond is cleared, damon_is_running(ctx) will
return true.

Will damon_call() block indefinitely on wait_for_completion() because the
thread is exiting and will never signal it, leaving the sysfs writer
permanently stuck in D state while holding the system-wide param_lock?

> +
> +	return err ? err : control.return_code;
>  }

[ ... ]
diff --git a/mm/damon/reclaim.c b/mm/damon/reclaim.c
--- a/mm/damon/reclaim.c
+++ b/mm/damon/reclaim.c
[ ... ]
> +static int damon_reclaim_commit_inputs_store(const char *val,
> +					     const struct kernel_param *kp)
> +{
[ ... ]
> +	/*
> +	 * Skip damon_call() if ctx is not initialized to avoid
> +	 * NULL pointer dereference.
> +	 */
> +	if (!ctx)
> +		return -EINVAL;
> +
> +	err = damon_call(ctx, &control);
> +
> +	return err ? err : control.return_code;
>  }

Are these same regressions present here in damon_reclaim_commit_inputs_store()
as well?
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Module Initialization Failure when providing `commit_inputs=Y` at boot or load time.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. The `commit_inputs_store` module parameter callback is executed by the kernel's `parse_args()` phase during module initialization, which occurs *before* `module_init()` runs.
2. The global `ctx` pointer is initialized within the module's `init` function (e.g., `damon_reclaim_init`), meaning `ctx` is guaranteed to be `NULL` when `parse_args()` fires.
3. The new callback explicitly checks `if (!ctx) return -EINVAL;`.
4. Returning an error code from a parameter setter aborts the entire module loading sequence. This completely breaks backward compatibility and prevents the kernel from loading the module if `commit_inputs=Y` is provided as a boot argument or via `/etc/modprobe.d/` configurations."},{"problem":"Writing `Y` to `commit_inputs` via sysfs when the monitor is inactive now fails with `-EINVAL`, breaking userspace ABI expectations.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. Previously, writing `Y` to `commit_inputs` while DAMON was disabled (`enabled=N`) was a valid operation that silently succeeded, naturally committing the parameters to be applied upon the next start.
2. The updated callback now unconditionally passes the request to `damon_call()`, which synchronously attempts to execute the control.
3. If the `kdamond` thread is not running, `damon_call()` detects `!damon_is_running(ctx)` and delegates to `damon_call_handle_inactive_ctx()`, which immediately strips the request and returns `-EINVAL`.
4. This strict synchronous dependency explicitly breaks backward compatibility for any existing userspace scripts or tools that configure and commit parameters before turning the monitor on."},{"problem":"Executing a synchronous `damon_call()` inside a module parameter callback stalls system-wide sysfs module parameter operations for up to the duration of the DAMON sampling interval.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. Sysfs writes to module parameters acquire a lock before executing the callback. For built-in modules, `kernel_param_lock(NULL)` acquires the global `param_lock` mutex.
2. The new `commit_inputs_store` callback invokes `damon_call()` synchronously. `damon_call()` blocks on `wait_for_completion()` waiting for the `kdamond` thread to process the control block.
3. The `kdamond_fn` worker thread only checks its call list once per interval and sleeps via `usleep_range()` without an explicit waitqueue or asynchronous wakeup mechanism.
4. Consequently, a user writing to `commit_inputs` will sleep for the entire `sample_interval` (which can be seconds) while holding `param_lock`. During this stall, no other task on the system can read or write any built-in module parameters, causing severe and unpredictable latency spikes system-wide."},{"problem":"A race condition between `damon_call()` and `kdamond` thread termination causes a permanent deadlock holding the global `param_lock`.","severity":"Critical","severity_explanation":"This problem wasn't introduced by this patch, but the patch catastrophically exposes it by tying it to `KPARAM_MUTEX` (the global `param_lock` for built-in modules). The race window occurs during `kdamond_fn()` exit:
1. `kdamond_fn()` flushes and completes pending controls via `kdamond_call(ctx, true)`.
2. A concurrent sysfs write triggers `damon_call()`, which acquires the `call_controls` lock, appends a new `control` block, and unlocks.
3. `damon_call()` checks `damon_is_running(ctx)`. Because `ctx->kdamond` has not yet been set to `NULL` (which happens a few lines later in `kdamond_fn()`), it incorrectly evaluates to `true`.
4. `damon_call()` calls `wait_for_completion()` and blocks indefinitely.
5. `kdamond_fn()` finishes its exit sequence, sets `ctx->kdamond = NULL`, and terminates. The completion is never signaled, leaving the sysfs writer permanently stuck in uninterruptible sleep (D state) while holding the system-wide `param_lock`."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 2fe50c13aebc88839d89dd3cdd5944c18c25dc9b
Author: Liew Rui Yan <aethernet65535@gmail.com>
